text: It's a highlight in the bullfighting calendar.
But for the first time in 35 years, it had to be suspended because of this.
The matador, David Moura, is thrown around like a rag doll,
suffering serious but not life-threatening injuries.
Shocking enough for one event, but then spectators watched in horror
as a second matador was tossed in the air and trampled.
Although the coring is serious, it heals after a while.
In Spain, about 2,000 bullfights are still held every year.
But in 2010, Catalonia became the second Spanish region
after the Canary Islands to ban the tradition.
Bullfighting is thought to have been popularized by the Romans
and dates back at least 4,000 years.
But last year, the tradition was granted cultural heritage status
in order to protect it from further bans.
But this matador is very matter-of-fact about the risks involved.
I hope the recovery won't take too long,
because in a month, I'm bullfighting in Seville.
Bullfighting is on the decline,
partly because of the controversy surrounding it.
Animal welfare groups say it's outdated and barbaric.
But Hardin supporters say bullfighting is an ancient art form.
Deeply rooted in Spanish heritage.
Alba Patel, BBC News.
